text: The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection of the sigmoid colon in a morbidly obese patient, revealing large epiploic appendages. The surgeon unfolds these appendages to expose the anti-mesenteric surface of the bowel.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, aiming to mobilize the bowel enough to elevate the rectosigmoid junction. Despite challenges, the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle is preserved, and the ureter is identified. The surgeon selects proximal and distal margins for the planned transrectal extraction of the specimen.
The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery at the desired proximal margin, marking the medial edge and scoring it down towards the distal margin at the rectosigmoid junction.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the sigmoid colon, which appears floppy and fatty, using existing trocars without re-docking or changing ports. The recto sigmoid junction is cleaned to gain additional length, and limited mobilization around the splenic flexure is performed.

The surgeon elevates the descending colon mesentery from the kidney, removing omental adhesions in the left upper quadrant to gain additional length. The colon is positioned to reach the pelvis easily. Both margins are checked for cleanliness before performing a perfusion assessment. The bowel wall is transected using a vessel sealer, avoiding the use of a stapler to minimize hernia risk.

The surgeon places the specimen off to the side and begins a single-layer sewn anastomosis of the bowel, starting at the posterior midline with full-thickness bites. A lateral suture is used for traction, and perfusion is checked again. The silk suture provides visible support during the procedure.

The surgeon switches the suture to the outside and begins a single-layer hand-sewn anastomosis, starting in the posterior midline. The sutures are tied together, and a second layer is added for additional security. The pelvis is flooded for a leak test, which shows no leakage. A rigid proctosigmoidoscopy confirms the integrity of the anastomosis.
